data.title: United States Antimony targets $125M 2026 revenue with expanded contracts and mining projects
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: United States Antimony Corporation (UAMY) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Gary Evans highlighted several major developments, including the acquisition of a tungsten property in Ontario, Canada, now holding an inferred resource value exceeding $4.6 billion. Evans stated, "We just announced several weeks ago that this particular property, after we've acquired it and done some work, has an inferred resource value now exceeding $4.6 billion, and that's B with a billion."
  • Evans announced an upgraded $248 million five-year sole-source antimony ingot contract with the U.S. government’s Defense Logistics Agency (DLA), anticipating $75 million in deliveries for fiscal 2026. The company also secured a five-year, $106.7 million contract with an undisclosed industrial customer for antimony used in flame retardants and industrial fabrics.
  • Evans introduced two new independent board members, General Jack Keane and John Marinelli, to strengthen governance and industry reach.
  • The company opened the Stibnite Hill Antimony mine in Montana and restarted its Madero Antimony smelter in Mexico, with plans to process more material in 2026. Evans noted the acquisition of a 10% stake in an Australian gold-antimony mine for $37.2 million.
  • CFO Richard Isaak reported, "Sales for 2025 were $39.3 million, up 163% over the prior year...gross profit dollars grew by $6.4 million or 185% over the prior year, and gross margin grew by 2 percentage points from 23% to 25% year-over-year."
  • Isaak also highlighted a cash balance of $91.3 million at year-end and working capital of $44.6 million, with antimony inventory rising from 78 tons to 465 tons year-over-year.
  • Evans emphasized continued expansion of mining claims in Alaska, calling the region "the heart of our future mining claims," and previewed potential from a recent acquisition there.
  • Lloyd Bardswich, Executive VP and Chief Mining Engineer, detailed the reopening and upgrade of Montana and Alaska mining operations, referencing a $297 million in-situ value at the McDonald Creek property and the technical report on the Fostung tungsten deposit in Ontario.
  • Melissa Pagen, President of Bear River Zeolite, outlined efforts to focus on water treatment and agriculture segments, planning for "dramatic increase in sales in 2026" and scaling bulk distribution to major customers.
  • Jonathan Miller, VP of Investor Relations, reported a share price increase of 182% to $5.02 and market cap expansion to $703 million, citing growing institutional ownership: "Institutional participation has scaled rapidly...we now have 222 institutional holders up from just 48 in Q4 2024."

Outlook

  • Evans reaffirmed 2026 revenue guidance of $125 million, noting, "We are very firm on our guidance of $125 million. We know how much we'll be delivering to the DLA. We have material in hand, material on the water."
  • Management signaled expectations of quarter-to-quarter volatility but projected meeting annual targets, with further government funding applications underway.
  • More government funding and additional project filings are expected, supported by a recent $27 million award from the U.S. Department of the Interior.

Financial Results

  • Sales in 2025 reached $39.3 million, up 163% year-over-year, with antimony sales rising due to price increases and zeolite sales due to higher prices and volumes.
  • Gross profit increased by $6.4 million or 185%, with gross margin improving from 23% to 25%.
  • Net loss widened to $4.3 million, including $6.7 million in noncash expenses, primarily stock compensation.
  • Cash position increased to $91.3 million, with working capital at $44.6 million and debt at $195,000.
  • Antimony inventory climbed to 465 tons, with 17% sourced from company-owned mines.

Q&A

  • Analyst asked about production mix evolution with expanded inventory and mining: Evans responded, "We would love to be mining all of our own antimony...having that low cost of ore is so important to guaranteeing your profit margins."
  • On equipment delays for the Thompson Falls expansion, Evans said, "Unfortunately not. This is very specialized equipment...we have to wait until they deliver."
  • Analyst inquired about Alaska ore ramp-up: Evans indicated expectations of "1,000 tonnes a month" but would "be happy with 200 tonnage a month," pending further exploration.
  • On defense demand, Evans confirmed, "We're already supporting them. That's the $248 million contract we won in September of last year."
  • Analyst questioned M&A appetite: Evans stated, "We look at both. We haven't seen anything on the M&A front that has really appealed to us...it's got to be within a year to 2 years for us to really get excited about it."
  • On exposure to battery manufacturing, Evans replied, "We do not have any long-term contracts, but those discussions are ongoing."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was persistent, seeking clarity on production, expansion timelines, and contract execution; questions often revisited inventory, supply chain, and growth risks, reflecting a slightly positive but vigilant stance.
  • Management's tone was confident and optimistic, emphasizing operational speed and strategic positioning, as Evans stated, "We are in a perfect position as being a first mover to capture a lot more business."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence remained strong but now more focused on execution and delivery timelines; analysts’ tone stayed constructive but pressed for specifics on operational risks and contract fulfillment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 held steady at $125 million in both calls, but management now emphasized "bumpy" quarter-to-quarter performance and readiness for volatility.
  • Strategic focus shifted to accelerated delivery, rapid scaling of mining operations, and further institutionalization, compared with previously highlighting contracts and vertical integration.
  • Key financial metrics improved, with sales, gross profit, and cash position all increasing sharply from Q3 to Q4.
  • Management sentiment evolved from forward-looking optimism to an execution-driven narrative, while analysts continued to probe operational risks and supply chain bottlenecks.
  • New board appointments and further expansion in Alaska and Canada reflect a heightened focus on governance and resource diversification.

Risks and Concerns

  • Major challenges include equipment delivery delays impacting the Thompson Falls expansion, third-party supplier risks, and weather-related disruptions in Alaska and Montana.
  • Evans acknowledged, "There's going to be quarters like, oh, that looks like not so good. And there's going to be quarters like, wow, they blew it out of the park."
  • Analysts raised concerns about inventory levels, production mix, exposure to Chinese supply shocks, and speed of ramp-up at new assets.
  • Management outlined mitigation through increased internal sourcing, diversified supply agreements, and ongoing applications for government funding.

Final Takeaway

United States Antimony Corporation ended 2025 with transformative growth, highlighted by major government and industrial contracts, substantial resource acquisitions, and strengthened financials. Management reaffirmed confidence in meeting its $125 million 2026 revenue target, citing expanded production capacity, growing institutional investor support, and new mining assets in Alaska and Canada. The company acknowledged short-term operational volatility but emphasized its unique strategic position as a critical minerals supplier and its drive for accelerated execution and long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Taysha outlines 2026 BLA submission path for TSHA-102 as pivotal trial enrollment advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Taysha Gene Therapies (TSHA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Sean Nolan highlighted 2025 as "a year of significant execution" for Taysha, calling attention to "compelling REVEAL Phase I/II data across pediatric, adolescent and adult patients with Rett syndrome treated with TSHA-102," as well as FDA breakthrough therapy designation and written alignment on REVEAL pivotal and ASPIRE trial designs. Nolan stated, "We remain on track to complete dosing in the second quarter of 2026," emphasizing the streamlined BLA path and commercial readiness efforts.
  • Nolan reported, "Multiple patients have now been dosed in the [REVEAL pivotal] trial with enrollment advancing across multiple sites."
  • The CEO described TSHA-102 as "generally well tolerated with no treatment-related serious adverse events or dose-limiting toxicities observed" to date.
  • TSHA-102 is positioned as addressing "the root cause of Rett syndrome" for an estimated 15,000 to 20,000 patients across the U.S., EU, and U.K. Nolan noted, "Market research indicated that clinicians and caregivers strongly prefer intrathecal administration over direct-to-brain CNS delivery."
  • The company announced the appointment of Brad Martin as Senior Vice President of Market Access and Value to "further strengthen our commercial leadership team" as Taysha builds toward potential commercialization.
  • President and Head of R&D Sukumar Nagendran said, "We presented data from Part A of the REVEAL Phase I/II trial last year, demonstrating a 100% response rate across our 10 treated patients in both close cohorts."
  • CFO Kamran Alam reported, "Research and development expenses were $86.4 million for the year ended December 31, 2025, compared to $66 million for the year ended December 31, 2024... Net loss for the year ended December 31, 2025, was $109 million or $0.34 per share compared to a net loss of $89.3 million or $0.36 per share for the year ended December 31, 2024. As of December 31, 2025, Taysha had $319.8 million in cash and cash equivalents."

Outlook

  • Management indicated the pivotal and ASPIRE trials are "on track to complete dosing in the second quarter of 2026."
  • The company expects to initiate BLA-enabling process performance qualification (PPQ) lots using the commercial manufacturing process in Q2 2026.
  • Nolan stated, "We expect that our current cash resources will be sufficient to fund planned operating expenses into 2028."
  • The company plans to share "additional details on our TSHA-102 commercial strategy in the second half of the year."

Financial Results

  • R&D expenses increased to $86.4 million for 2025, with the rise attributed to "higher compensation expenses due to increased research and development headcount" and expanded clinical and manufacturing activities.
  • General and administrative expenses were $33.9 million for the year, with the increase driven by higher compensation, legal fees, and debt issuance costs related to the 2025 Trinity Term Loan.
  • Taysha raised $50 million in gross proceeds during Q4 through its ATM equity offering program to support a potential commercial inventory build in 2027.
  • Net loss for 2025 was $109 million or $0.34 per share.
  • Cash and cash equivalents stood at $319.8 million at year-end 2025.

Q&A

  • Kristen Kluska, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about community preference for intrathecal administration and its understanding. Nolan responded, "The most obvious is I think everyone can relate to a lumbar puncture... it's easy for us to do this lumbar puncture... in terms of being able to broaden the reach... it's a much easier route of administration."
  • Salveen Richter, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about Brad Martin's priorities and expectations for the Part A data update. Nolan explained, "You'll see data on all 12 Part A patients... and we'll have a minimum of 12 months of data on all patients... What we hope to show is what we've been able to demonstrate to date, which is that the early improvements are sustained."
  • Biren Amin, Piper Sandler: Sought clarity on BLA, PPQ lot timing, and filing scenarios. Alam confirmed PPQ lots "will be completed by end of this year." Nolan added, "We can do the analysis, the interim analysis once all patients dosed in the pivotal are at 6 months... we would sit down with the FDA, go through that data with them and work to align with them on what the next steps could potentially be."
  • Tazeen Ahmad, BofA Securities: Asked about commercial opportunity in Europe after recent regulatory events. Nolan said, "I don't think there is a read-through based on what happened to Acadia... we're able to demonstrate restoration of function in a neurodevelopmental disease... I think we'll be able to capture the appropriate value."
  • Additional questions focused on data pooling, milestone analysis, patient enrollment progress, and the company's approach to regulatory engagement.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a generally positive tone, highlighting progress on enrollment, regulatory alignment, and clinical outcomes, but asked for specifics on timelines, commercial strategies, and clinical data updates.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout, with Nolan stating, "We are confident that our CMC activities are on track to support our planned BLA submission."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst questions in Q4 were more focused on execution details, while management appeared more assertive on timelines and regulatory progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter emphasized completion of pivotal and ASPIRE trial dosing in Q2 2026, FDA alignment on pooling of clinical and commercial data for BLA submission, and cash runway into 2028, whereas the previous quarter focused on the initiation of pivotal trials and receipt of Breakthrough Therapy designation.
  • The Q4 call provided greater detail on commercial preparations, including the appointment of a new SVP of Market Access and Value, and advanced manufacturing readiness.
  • Analysts in the current quarter probed further into operational execution and regulatory strategies, reflecting a shift from previous questions on foundational clinical and regulatory alignment.
  • Management's tone in Q4 was notably more confident regarding regulatory timelines and commercial strategy buildout.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that "clinical trial and GMP expenses" were increasing due to scaling activities and highlighted the pivotal importance of maintaining FDA alignment on trial protocols and CMC requirements.
  • The company acknowledged the limitations of currently approved therapies for Rett syndrome and the urgency for new options, but did not identify new business risks in the current quarter beyond previously disclosed regulatory, operational, and competitive challenges.
  • Analysts sought clarity on potential regulatory outcomes, commercial pricing in Europe, and durability of efficacy data, indicating continued vigilance on execution and market access risks.

Final Takeaway

Taysha Gene Therapies reported substantial progress in 2025, with pivotal and ASPIRE trial dosing on track for completion in Q2 2026, strengthened regulatory alignment with the FDA, and a clear commercial strategy underway for TSHA-102. The company’s financial position, bolstered by a recent capital raise, supports operations into 2028, while ongoing clinical data updates and manufacturing readiness are expected to drive momentum toward a BLA submission and potential product launch for Rett syndrome.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: KNOT Offshore Partners says buyout talks terminated
data.description:
KNOT Offshore Partners (KNOP) down 8.9% post-market Thursday after saying talks about an offer from Knutsen NYK Offshore Tankers to buy the company have been terminated.

KNOT Offshore (KNOP) said a board committee composed of directors who are unaffiliated with Knutsen had retained independent legal and financial advisors to evaluate the offer and engaged with the company about the proposal, but the parties determined they will not be able to reach an agreement and have ended discussions.

In November, Knutsen submitted an unsolicited offer to buy all KNOT Offshore (KNOP) publicly traded units for $10 each in cash.

data.title: Movado signals margin expansion path as Q4 sales rise 5.6% with strong U.S. growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Movado Group (MOV) Q4 2026

Management View

  • Efraim Grinberg, Chairman & CEO, reported that "revenue increased 2.7% to $671.3 million and adjusted operating income grew 28.7% to $34.8 million, reflecting strong execution across our strategic priorities." He noted sales in the fourth quarter rose 5.6% to $191.6 million, led by the U.S. wholesale and retail business. The company ended the year with $230 million in cash and no debt. Grinberg emphasized, "We are seeing increased participation from younger consumers, along with a strong return of women into the category, driven by smaller case sizes and jewelry-inspired designs and fresh styling." The Movado brand saw wholesale sales grow over 25% and e-commerce increase 18% in the quarter. Grinberg highlighted a focus on higher-margin products and reducing promotional activity, stating, "We see a clear path to margin expansion over time as we continue to execute against these priorities."
  • Sallie DeMarsilis, Executive VP, COO & CFO, stated, "For the fourth quarter of fiscal 2026, sales were $191.6 million as compared to $181.5 million last year, reflecting growth in our owned brands, licensed brands and in our company stores." She added, "We held gross margin nearly flat at 54.1% of sales as compared to 54.2% in the fourth quarter of last year."

Outlook

  • Management did not provide a fiscal 2027 outlook due to "current economic and geopolitical uncertainty, including the unpredictable impact of the current Middle East conflict and ongoing tariff developments." Grinberg indicated that "initiatives are clearly focused on improving profitability" and noted ongoing investments in brand positioning, higher-margin product mix, and operational efficiency.

Financial Results

  • For the fourth quarter, sales were $191.6 million, up from $181.5 million in the prior year period. U.S. net sales increased 11.2%, while international net sales rose 1%. Gross margin was 54.1% of sales. Operating expenses were $89.3 million, driven by higher performance-based compensation but partially offset by reduced marketing expenses. Operating income increased to $14.4 million from $13.5 million a year ago. Net income for the quarter was $13 million, or $0.57 per diluted share, compared to $11.5 million, or $0.51 per diluted share, in the year-ago period. Cash at year-end was $230.5 million.
  • For the fiscal year, sales reached $671.3 million and operating income was $34.8 million. The company repurchased approximately 208,000 shares in fiscal 2026, with $46.1 million remaining authorized for buybacks.

Q&A

  • Owen Rickert, Northland Capital Markets, asked about the drivers of the 18% movado.com growth in Q4 and the long-term D2C mix. Grinberg responded that it was due to "a higher level of engagement from consumers and the connection that Movado is making them with new innovation and shapes and sizes across our product segments."
  • Rickert questioned the breakdown of U.S. net sales growth between volume and price. Grinberg indicated it was "mostly volume driven," with only minimal price increases to offset tariffs.
  • Rickert inquired about the basis point impact of tariffs on gross margin. DeMarsilis said, "The IEEPA tariffs this past fiscal year hurt us in our cost of goods sold by about $10 million. In basis points for the year, it was 150 basis points... the fourth quarter was impacted by about 180 basis points in gross margin."
  • Rickert asked about accelerating share repurchases. Grinberg replied, "We are always very prudent with our cash balances and want to make sure that the dividend is solid... we try to offset dilution with our share repurchases."
  • Hamed Khorsand, BWS Financial, asked if benefits from revised Swiss tariffs would help this year. Grinberg said, "I don't think we will see a major benefit this year because we didn't bring in a lot of inventory at those types of tariff, only really on things that we needed to have in a timely manner."
  • Khorsand questioned if wholesale growth was due to inventory catch-up or demand. Grinberg stated, "it was really driven by demand... we still have retailers right now chasing inventory."
  • Khorsand inquired about operational efficiency from increased production. Grinberg answered, "as volume increases, it should help to leverage our gross margin and cost of goods sold."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed the company on online sales drivers, tariff impacts, and supply chain efficiency, indicating a neutral to slightly positive tone, with interest in underlying demand and margin management.
  • Management maintained a positive and confident tone in prepared remarks, highlighting strong execution, margin discipline, and brand momentum, but was more cautious when addressing the outlook and tariff uncertainties. Grinberg used phrases like "we are well positioned for continued growth" and "we see a clear path to margin expansion," but also flagged "volatility around tariffs."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained confident on execution and brand strategy, with slightly increased caution due to geopolitical and tariff uncertainties. Analyst tone remained inquisitive with a focus on margin levers and demand sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained cautious, with management again electing not to provide a forward outlook due to uncertainty—this stance was unchanged from the previous quarter.
  • Strategic focus continued on innovation, consumer engagement, and margin expansion, but the current quarter emphasized operational efficiency and inventory management in response to ongoing demand.
  • Key metrics showed fourth quarter sales and profits improved sequentially and year-over-year, with a notable increase in U.S. sales and e-commerce performance.
  • Management sentiment was consistent, highlighting improved profitability and brand momentum, but with heightened attention to macroeconomic and geopolitical risks. Analyst focus persisted on drivers of growth, tariff effects, and operational leverage.

Risks and Concerns

  • Grinberg noted "the conflict in the Middle East has introduced additional uncertainty in global markets" and ongoing volatility in tariff rates.
  • DeMarsilis explained, "there's still a lot of volatility around tariffs because there's a new statute... but that is above the current duty rates."
  • Management highlighted a lack of fiscal 2027 guidance due to these risks and reiterated focus on cost management, supply chain efficiency, and inventory control to mitigate uncertainty.

Final Takeaway

Movado Group closed fiscal 2026 with renewed growth and strong execution in key U.S. and digital channels, while maintaining margin discipline and a robust cash position. The company’s focus on younger consumers, product innovation, and operational efficiency supported improved profitability. However, management underscored a cautious stance for fiscal 2027, citing global uncertainties and volatile tariffs, and chose not to issue formal guidance while emphasizing ongoing investments in brand strength and margin expansion initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Curis targets full enrollment in PCNSL registrational study within 12–18 months while advancing CLL proof-of-concept
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Curis, Inc. (CRIS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • James Dentzer, President and CEO, stated that "we continue to make steady progress in our TakeAim Lymphoma study in primary CNS lymphoma, one of the most rare and most difficult to treat of the NHL subtypes." The registrational study is designed to support accelerated submissions in both the U.S. and Europe, with enrollment progressing and ongoing support from investigators and regulatory authorities.
  • Dentzer highlighted the strategic expansion into chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), noting the company is activating sites in the U.S. and Europe for a proof-of-concept study. "We expect to have initial data at the ASH Annual Meeting in December."
  • For acute myeloid leukemia (AML), Dentzer recapped results from the ongoing triplet study, reporting that "5 of 8 evaluable patients were able to achieve MRD conversion."
  • Diantha Duvall, CFO, reported, "Curis reported net income of $19.4 million or $1.23 per share for the fourth quarter of 2025 as compared to a net loss of $9.6 million or $1.25 per share for the same period in 2024." Duvall explained the income was due to a $27.2 million onetime noncash gain from the sale of Erivedge.

Outlook

  • Management indicated the TakeAim Lymphoma study in PCNSL is on track for full enrollment within 12 to 18 months, with anticipated data and potential regulatory submissions following patient follow-up.
  • The company is advancing its proof-of-concept study in CLL, with initial data expected at the ASH Annual Meeting in December.
  • Duvall stated, "Curis' cash and cash equivalents as of December 31, 2025, together with initial gross proceeds of $20.2 million received in January 2026 and expected gross proceeds of up to an additional $20.2 million... should enable our planned operations into the second half of 2027."
  • Duvall confirmed, "We'll have no meaningful revenue. The revenue effectively ended in November of 2025."

Financial Results

  • Curis reported net income of $19.4 million for Q4 2025, attributed to a $27.2 million onetime noncash gain from the sale of Erivedge. For the year ended December 31, 2025, Curis reported a net loss of $7.6 million.
  • Research and development expenses were $5.8 million for the fourth quarter and $28.3 million for the year ended December 31, 2025. General and administrative expenses were $2.9 million for the quarter and $14 million for the year.
  • Duvall stated the decreases in expenses were "primarily attributable to lower manufacturing, employee-related and clinical costs" for R&D, and lower employee-related costs for G&A.

Q&A

  • Srikripa Devarakonda, Truist Securities: Asked about trial prioritization between PCNSL, CLL, and AML. Dentzer explained, "We are definitely prioritizing NHL ahead of AML. Right now... we're pushing forward very aggressively in PCNSL... As well as CLL... the bulk of our spend is going towards PCNSL."
  • Yale Jen, Laidlaw & Company: Inquired about PCNSL enrollment and revenue expectations. Dentzer responded that enrollment is "on track for what we've suggested," with the process being variable but "everything is going according to plan." Duvall confirmed "we'll have no meaningful revenue" for 2026.
  • Daniel Bronder, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about expected CLL data at ASH 2026 and success metrics. Dentzer said, "this is more about our plans and our expectations at this point," and Dr. Hamdy added, "we are trying to change the CLL treatment paradigm... we're aiming hopefully to deepen that response and see that patients are going in the right direction towards a complete remission and hopefully, MRD-negative."
  • Bronder followed up on patient dosing in CLL. Dentzer replied, "we haven't disclosed that yet. What we are saying for now is that we are in the process of initiating the study."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions about resource prioritization, enrollment timelines, revenue outlook, and clinical success metrics, with a generally neutral to slightly positive tone, acknowledging progress and expressing interest in upcoming data. One noted, "congrats with the good balance sheet and advance the programs forward."
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone in prepared remarks, frequently highlighting progress and future milestones. In Q&A, the tone was consistent, focusing on execution and resource efficiency, with phrases such as "we're very thoughtful about how we're prioritizing our resources" and "I hope that longer explanation is helpful."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence has increased, especially with the improved financial position and clear prioritization of PCNSL and CLL. Analysts’ tone remained inquisitive but supportive.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw the completion of the Erivedge sale and a one-time noncash gain, a significant shift from the prior period’s recurring losses.
  • Guidance language is now more concrete, with clearer timelines for PCNSL enrollment and CLL data disclosure, compared to the prior quarter's focus on study initiation and protocol submission.
  • Resource prioritization has shifted more explicitly toward PCNSL and CLL, with AML activities deprioritized until additional cash is raised.
  • The previous quarter underscored the need for additional capital, whereas the current call emphasized an improved cash position enabling operations into the second half of 2027.
  • Analysts’ questions this quarter focused on trial execution and revenue visibility, reflecting a response to the company’s evolving strategic priorities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the inherent difficulty in enrolling PCNSL patients, noting variability in monthly enrollment and projecting full enrollment within a 12–18 month window.
  • Revenue stream from Erivedge has ended, with Duvall confirming "we'll have no meaningful revenue."
  • Resource allocation remains tightly managed, with AML deprioritized until further funding is secured.
  • Analysts sought clarity on data timelines and success metrics, particularly for CLL, and raised questions about the potential for meaningful near-term revenue.

Final Takeaway

Curis is advancing its registrational PCNSL study with a targeted full enrollment within 12 to 18 months and is initiating its proof-of-concept CLL study, with initial data expected by year-end. The company’s improved balance sheet, following the Erivedge sale and recent financings, supports operations into the second half of 2027. Management has prioritized resource allocation for PCNSL and CLL, while AML activities are on hold pending further funding, and no meaningful revenue is expected in the near term. Investors can expect updates on CLL progress at the ASH Annual Meeting in December and continued focus on executing the PCNSL registrational program.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold and silver sink on worries over inflation, tight central bank policy outlook
data.description:
Gold and silver futures plunged Thursday, as spiking energy prices stemming from the Middle East war sparked market concerns over inflation, quashing the likelihood that central banks will reduce interest rates.

Benchmark Brent crude prices jumped above $110/bbl after Iran attacked energy facilities across the Middle East following Israel's strike on its South ​Pars gas field.

Nearly all the world's major central banks, including the U.S. and Japan, kept rates unchanged this week while emphasizing their readiness to act to curb inflation if the energy shock caused by the war drives a broader surge in ​prices.

"Gold is now a very widely held position for institutional investors, ⁠and that has been on the back of the debasement trade over the last year. But the foundations of that trade are now weakening," TD Securities commodity strategist Daniel Ghali said in a note.

"For the near term, we continue to see risk to the downside," Ghali wrote. "There is a very substantial amount of room for gold to sell off while maintaining its bull market era trend support."

Gold has declined 12% since the start of the conflict, but silver has slumped 23.5% during that time, as metals used in industrial manufacturing are hit hard on concerns over the impact on China's manufacturing sector from the Middle East energy disruption.

Analysts say silver's surge in preceding months was based partly on expectations for increased demand for the metals from semiconductor makers, so potential limits to China's industrial output are weighing on prices.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for March delivery closed down 5.9% to $4,600.70/oz, its lowest settlement value since January 16, and front-month Comex March silver (XAGUSD:CUR) fell for the seventh straight session, finishing down 8.2% to $70.902/oz.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Four Corners Property Trust buys Panera Bread property for $3.8M
data.description:
• Four Corners Property Trust (FCPT) said on Thursday it acquired a Panera Bread property in Kentucky for $3.8 million.
  • The company said the asset is located in a high-traffic corridor and is corporate-operated under a triple net lease with about six years remaining.
  • The transaction was priced at a 6.7% capitalization rate on rent at closing, excluding transaction costs.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Realty Income, Apollo form $1B joint venture for retail properties
data.description:
Realty Income (O) on Thursday said it ​will get $1B from asset manager ‌Apollo Global Management (APO) and its affiliates for a 49% interest in a new joint venture that is expected to own portfolio of single-tenant retail properties with long-term net leases. 

Realty Income said it will continue to manage the portfolio, which includes about 500 retail properties across ‌retail ⁠sectors including dollar stores, grocery, quick service restaurants, drug stores and health & fitness.

Realty Income will retain the right to exercise a call option to redeem Apollo's equity interest after year seven and through year 15 of the joint venture, with the future call price calculated to ensure a capped IRR of 6.875% to Apollo during its ownership period.

The transaction is expected to close on March 31, 2026.

data.title: Alibaba targets $100B in AI and cloud revenue within five years amid accelerated AI investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) Q3 2026

Management View

  • CEO Yongming Wu stated that Alibaba maintained strong investment momentum in AI plus cloud and consumption, emphasizing that "Cloud Intelligence Group revenue growth accelerated to 36%, while our Quick Commerce business continued to expand in scale with ongoing improvement in unit economics." Wu outlined a clear business goal: "Over the next 5 years, our goal is to surpass USD 100 billion in combined cloud and AI external revenue, including MaaS."
  • Wu detailed that "Cloud Intelligence Group's market share has grown for 3 consecutive quarters, rising to 36% with our lead continuing to widen." He further highlighted that "Alibaba Cloud's cumulative external revenue through February for fiscal year 2026 officially surpassed RMB 100 billion."
  • Wu described the launch of Alibaba Token Hub Business Group as "the organizational foundation for executing Alibaba's AI strategy and the hub for efficient coordination across our AI businesses."
  • Wu reported that "T-Head's proprietary GPU chips have achieved scaled mass production. As of February 2026, T-Head had cumulatively shipped 470,000 AI chips... more than 60% of the T-Head ships serve external customers."
  • CFO Toby Xu stated, "Total revenue was RMB 284.8 billion, excluding revenue from Sun Art and Intime revenue on a like-for-like basis have grown by 9%. Total adjusted EBITA decreased by 57% primarily due to our strategic investments in technology-related innovation initiatives and the consumption front, including quick commerce business."
  • Xu added, "Our GAAP net income was RMB 15.6 billion, a decrease of 66%. Operating cash flow was an inflow of RMB 36 billion. Free cash flow was RMB 11.3 billion, a decrease of RMB 27.7 billion from the same quarter last year. We are reinvesting our cash flow to be a leader in AI and quick commerce."

Outlook

  • Wu communicated a five-year external revenue target: "Over the next 5 years, our goal is to surpass USD 100 billion in combined cloud and AI external revenue, including MaaS."
  • Wu said, "We expect MaaS to become Cloud Intelligence Group's largest revenue product."
  • Management expects quick commerce business to be profitable in FY '29, maintaining the target of achieving over RMB 1 trillion in Quick Commerce GMV by FY '28.

Financial Results

  • Xu reported, "Total revenue was RMB 284.8 billion... revenue from China e-commerce group was RMB 159.3 billion, an increase of 6%. Customer management revenue increased by 1%."
  • Quick commerce business revenue rose 56% to RMB 20.8 billion, while adjusted EBITA for Alibaba China E-commerce Group was RMB 34.6 billion, a decrease of 43% due to investment in quick commerce and technology.
  • Cloud Intelligence Group revenue from external customers grew 35%. Xu noted, "AI-related products continue to lead this momentum. We delivered our tenth consecutive quarter of triple-digit growth in AI revenue."
  • Xu stated, "All other segment revenue decreased by 25% to RMB 67.3 billion, mainly due to the disposal of Sun Art and Intime businesses as well as a decrease in revenue from Cainiao."

Q&A

  • Robin Zhu, Bernstein: Asked about how Token Hub will change the integration of cloud and AI businesses. Wu responded that "the need to achieve this tight integration between application and model" is the critical priority and that "our top priority absolutely is to develop the most intelligent models."
  • Joyce Ju, BofA Securities: Inquired about CMR trends and consumer sentiment. Wu indicated that "physical goods GMV and CMR trend have significantly recovered from the December quarter, and EBITA is expected to improve accordingly."
  • Gary Yu, Morgan Stanley: Sought priorities between market share and unit economics for quick commerce. Wu explained that "while growing our market share, we have continued to significantly improve UE... we maintain our target of achieving over RMB 1 trillion in Quick Commerce GMV by FY '28."
  • Alicia Yap, Citigroup: Questioned the T-Head chip business and potential spin-off. Wu clarified, "T-Head is ranked in the top tier of the domestic AI chip ecosystem... over the past 2 years, we've successfully commercialized and launched chips with total volume exceeding 470,000 units with annual revenue reaching the RMB 10 billion level." He added, "we don't rule out T-Head considering an IPO in the future, although we currently do not have any definitive time line."
  • Yuan Liao, Citic: Asked about the 5-year AI and cloud revenue target. Wu emphasized confidence in surpassing $100 billion and described major growth drivers as MaaS, enterprise-level inference and training, and traditional cloud computing.
  • Alex Yao, JPMorgan: Queried about the 3-year investment cycle in e-commerce. Wu stated, "We will continue to invest heavily over the next two years in order to achieve our goal of surpassing RMB 1 trillion in quick commerce sales."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised probing questions on integration strategy, monetization, consumer sentiment, chip business prospects, and profitability timelines, signaling a neutral to slightly positive tone as they acknowledged progress but pressed for greater detail.
  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking tone in prepared remarks, with Wu repeatedly highlighting strategic clarity, growth targets, and investment commitment. In the Q&A, Wu and Xu provided detailed responses but sometimes emphasized the long-term nature of returns and the need for ongoing investment, reflecting slight defensiveness.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone was similar, maintaining focus on execution risks and long-term returns. Management's tone shifted from emphasis on scaling and efficiency to aggressive investment and bold five-year targets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted this quarter to explicit five-year revenue targets for AI and cloud, compared to prior focus on near-term growth and efficiency.
  • Strategic focus intensified on full-stack AI and Token Hub integration, supported by ongoing quick commerce investment.
  • Management's confidence in achieving AI and cloud scale was more pronounced, with large-scale chip production milestones and cloud market share gains highlighted.
  • Key metrics such as total revenue and cloud segment growth remained robust but adjusted EBITA and net income showed larger declines due to higher investment spend, a trend also visible last quarter.
  • Analysts continued to probe for details on monetization, strategic timelines, investment returns, and business integration, reflecting consistent concerns across both quarters.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "intense competition and significant investment in user experience" as reasons for fluctuating adjusted EBITA in core e-commerce.
  • Wu acknowledged "weak macro consumption, a warm winter, the later timing of the Chinese New Year" as factors challenging recent growth.
  • Analysts questioned the timeline for chip business profitability, sustainability of large-scale investment, and integration of AI with cloud and e-commerce.
  • Wu noted the "unique circumstances currently facing the AI industry in China" and the potential for "global AI computing power [to] be an extremely short supply."

Final Takeaway

Alibaba’s management is focused on long-term leadership in AI, cloud, and quick commerce, targeting more than $100 billion in external AI and cloud revenue over five years. Substantial investment in proprietary AI chips, integration of Token Hub, and expansion of quick commerce underpin this strategy, even as near-term profitability is pressured by technology and market investments. Management remains confident that these investments will drive future growth and market leadership, positioning Alibaba at the forefront of China’s digital and AI transformation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Kalshi raises over $1B, resulting in a $22B valuation - report
data.description:
Kalshi (KALSHI), the closely held predictions market platform, raised more than $1B in a funding round that gives it a valuation double that of its last financing round, according to a media report on Thursday.

The latest funding round, led by Coatue Management, values the company at $22B, compared with its December 2025 funding round that put its value at $11B, Bloomberg News reported, citing a person familiar with the matter.

The company's annual run rate is currently $1.5B, the person said. Kalshi declined to comment to Seeking Alpha about the fundraising.

The fundraising demonstrates investor appetite for exposure to the rapidly growing prediction market despite the increased regulatory scrutiny on the sector. Earlier this week, Arizona's attorney general filed criminal charges against Kalshi for allegedly running an "illegal gambling operation." Kalshi filed a federal lawsuit seeking to block the state from enforcing state anti-gambling laws against its CFTC-regulated sports events contracts.

The Wall Street Journal originally reported the latest funding round.
